ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for soda cracker; SNOMED CT coverage of generic snack foods is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of soda crackers is approximately 15 g (about 5 standard crackers), commonly consumed as a snack or alongside soup.

nutrient_source.title: Soda cracker — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for Soda cracker (F11080063). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 74 (glucose=100 reference) for soda crackers / saltine crackers is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 74 × 9.855 / 100 ≈ 7.3 (available carbohydrate per 15 g serving = 65.7 × 0.15 = 9.855 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 595 mg per 100 g with minimal potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit soda cracker intake; a single 15 g serving delivers approximately 89 mg sodium, and cumulative snacking can rapidly approach or exceed the recommended daily sodium ceiling of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 74) and high refined carbohydrate load (65.7 g/100 g) with very low dietary fibre (1.61 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit soda crackers due to their high GI and low fibre content, which cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; if consumed, pair with a protein or healthy fat source to partially blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~7.5 g/100 g), estimated trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g from vegetable shortening), and high sodium (595 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should minimise soda cracker consumption because the combination of saturated fat, trans fat, and high sodium collectively raises LDL cholesterol and blood pressure, both established CVD risk factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g) and saturated fat (~7.5 g/100 g) exceed RA thresholds; refined carbohydrates promote inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid soda crackers made with partially hydrogenated vegetable oil or shortening, as trans fat and saturated fat are pro-inflammatory and may exacerbate joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Soda cracker is a plain, light, and crispy baked biscuit leavened primarily with baking soda (sodium bicarbonate) and sometimes a small amount of yeast. It is made from refined wheat flour, vegetable shortening or oil, salt, and baking soda, resulting in a thin, flaky, mildly salty cracker with a neutral flavour and a characteristic airy, layered texture. Soda crackers are widely consumed as a snack on their own or paired with cheese, peanut butter, soup, or dips. They are a globally recognised product sold under brand names such as Nabisco Premium Saltines and are commonly available in Singapore supermarkets. Nutritionally, soda crackers are energy-dense at 476 kcal per 100 g, dominated by refined carbohydrates (65.7 g/100 g) and fat (20.06 g/100 g), with moderate protein (8.9 g/100 g) and very low dietary fibre (1.61 g/100 g). Sodium is high at 595 mg/100 g. The glycemic index is 74 (high), making these crackers unsuitable for blood glucose management. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 7.5 g/100 g and trans fat at approximately 0.3 g/100 g when made with vegetable shortening, raising cardiovascular and inflammatory concerns. Calcium is notably present at 167 mg/100 g. Soda crackers are not recommended for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to their high sodium, high GI, and unfavourable fat profile.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in soda crackers?
Are saltine crackers the same as soda crackers?
Is soda cracker good for hypertension?
Can I eat crackers with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macros, secondary nutrients, and calcium are sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database entry F11080063 at confidence 0.95. GI value of 74 is from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Saturated fat and trans fat estimates are derived from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable soda cracker products (USDA SR Legacy item 18228) and are documented as estimates. Potassium estimate is from USDA SR Legacy for saltine crackers.
notes: iron_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source (F11080063); this is treated as a confirmed zero per the HPB database entry — iron content is negligible or below detection threshold in this formulation. saturated_fat_g (7.5 g/100 g) and trans_fat_g (0.3 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 18228 (Crackers, saltines) as the HPB source does not report fatty acid breakdown; these are documented as estimates. potassium_mg (120 mg/100 g) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy entry 18228 as the HPB source does not report potassium. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed values are not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this entry and were not available in a directly matching approved secondary source at the time of processing. This is a generic Western snack food with no mandatory multilingual naming requirement beyond English; local_names fields are null accordingly.
